title: Cartels & Leniency
content:
Session 3: Cartels and leniency [¬ß2(c); ¬ß46 and Lesser Penalty Regulations] 
Samir Agrawal v CCI [2020 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Samir Agrawal, an independent legal practitioner, filed information before the CCI alleging cartelisation by drivers of Ola 
and Uber. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The allegation was premised on algorithmic pricing used by Ola and Uber, which: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Prevented riders from negotiating fares with drivers; 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Prevented drivers from offering discounts or competing on price; 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Imposed a uniform price determined by the platform‚Äôs algorithm. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč According to the informant: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The algorithm artificially manipulated supply and demand; 
‚ěĘ‚Äč It guaranteed higher fares to drivers who would otherwise compete with one another; 
‚ěĘ‚Äč This resulted in price fixation and restriction of competition. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Alleged violation: Section 3(1) read with Section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act, 2002. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The informant argued that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Ola and Uber acted as the ‚Äúhub‚ÄĚ, and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The drivers constituted the ‚Äúspokes‚ÄĚ, collectively forming a hub-and-spoke cartel. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The argument relied heavily on foreign antitrust jurisprudence, particularly a US class action (Spencer Meyer v. 
Travis Kalanick). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Procedural History 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs Findings ‚Üí no agreement or cartelisation was established. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Drivers: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Are independent service providers; 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Do not communicate or coordinate with each other; 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Do not share pricing information inter se. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Pricing: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Is determined by the algorithm based on dynamic factors such as: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Time of day, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Demand-supply fluctuations, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Weekday/weekend variations. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
No meeting of minds was shown. 
95 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hub-and-spoke theory rejected: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Ola and Uber were not facilitating collusion among drivers. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
There was no third-party intermediary enabling horizontal coordination. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 4 (abuse of dominance) ‚Üí no dominant position established ‚áí hence, no inquiry under Section 4 was 
warranted. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT‚Äôs Findings 
‚Ė†‚Äč
On Locus Standi ‚Üí held that Samir Agrawal had no locus standi. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Reason: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
No invasion of his legal rights; 
‚óŹ‚Äč
He was merely an independent advocate with no personal injury. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
On Merits ‚Üí upheld CCI‚Äôs findings: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
No price-fixing; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No connectivity between drivers; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No dominance. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Strongly rejected reliance on foreign jurisprudence: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
US antitrust standards cannot be mechanically imported; 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Indian competition law operates in a different statutory and economic context. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Held that it was contradictory to allege driver collusion while simultaneously claiming platform-imposed 
pricing. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the alleged conduct amounted to a ‚Äúhub and spoke cartel‚ÄĚ under the Competition Act, 2002. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether there was an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ or meeting of minds between drivers to fix prices. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether algorithmic pricing by aggregators constitutes cartelisation under Section 3. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Samir Agrawal had locus standi: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč As an informant before the CCI under Section 19, and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč As a ‚Äúperson aggrieved‚ÄĚ entitled to file an appeal before the NCLAT under Section 53B. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Statutory Provisions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(c) ‚ÄĒ Definition of ‚Äúcartel‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(1) ‚ÄĒ Prohibition of agreements causing AAEC. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(3)(a) ‚ÄĒ Presumption of AAEC in price-fixing agreements. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 19(1) ‚ÄĒ Power of CCI to inquire into contraventions on: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Information received from any person, or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Suo motu basis. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 35 ‚ÄĒ Right of legal representation. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 53B ‚ÄĒ Appeal to NCLAT by ‚Äúany person aggrieved‚ÄĚ. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 53N ‚ÄĒ Compensation for loss or damage caused by contravention. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(l) ‚ÄĒ Definition of ‚Äúperson‚ÄĚ (very wide and inclusive). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(f) ‚ÄĒ Definition of ‚Äúconsumer‚ÄĚ (comparatively narrow). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 45 ‚ÄĒ Penalty for furnishing false or misleading information. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulatory Framework 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI (General) Regulations, 2009, particularly Regulation 10: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Requires disclosure of facts and alleged contravention; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Does not require proof of personal injury or grievance. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Locus Standi under Section 19 
96 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court emphasised the in rem nature of competition law proceedings. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Proceedings under the Act are in public interest, not merely to vindicate private rights. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 19(1) originally referred to ‚Äúcomplaints‚ÄĚ, but post-2007 Amendment, this was replaced by ‚Äúinformation‚ÄĚ. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Significance of the 2007 Amendment: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
A ‚Äúcomplaint‚ÄĚ implies grievance; 
‚óŹ‚Äč
‚ÄúInformation‚ÄĚ may come from any person, regardless of personal harm. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Court contrasted: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
‚ÄúPerson‚ÄĚ under s.2(l) ‚ÄĒ extremely wide; 
‚óŹ‚Äč
‚ÄúConsumer‚ÄĚ under s.2(f) ‚ÄĒ narrow and transactional. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CCI‚Äôs power to act suo motu further reinforces the absence of strict locus requirements. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 45 acts as a safeguard against frivolous or mala fide informants. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulation 10 does not require the informant to show personal loss. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí NCLAT‚Äôs narrow interpretation of Section 19 was incorrect and set aside. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč ‚ÄúPerson Aggrieved‚ÄĚ under Section 53B must be interpreted broadly 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI and NCLAT deal with practices affecting competition and consumer welfare at large. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Act is concerned with market correction, not individual redress alone. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Distinguished Section 53B from Section 53N: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 53N(3) explicitly links compensation to loss or damage suffered; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 53B does not impose such a restriction. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Relied on CCI v. SAIL: CCI performs inquisitorial, investigative, and adjudicatory functions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, an informant before the CCI can qualify as a ‚Äúperson aggrieved‚ÄĚ for the purpose of appeal. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Samir Agrawal had the right to appeal, even though he ultimately failed on merits. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Hub and Spoke Cartel 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court upheld concurrent findings of CCI and NCLAT: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No facilitation of cartelisation by Ola or Uber. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Drivers act independently. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Algorithmic pricing ‚Č† agreement. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Price outcomes are dynamic and context-dependent. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Absence of communication or coordination between drivers. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court declined to interfere with factual findings. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Abuse of dominance: Though hypothetically possible, it was not pleaded, hence not examined. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On Locus Standi: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Samir Agrawal had valid locus standi: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
As an informant under Section 19, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
As a ‚Äúperson aggrieved‚ÄĚ under Section 53B. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On Merits: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No hub-and-spoke cartel existed. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No violation of Section 3 was established. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Outcome: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Appeal dismissed on merits. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Important jurisprudence laid down on locus standi and nature of competition proceedings. 
 
Ceat Ltd v CCI [2022 | NCLAT] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The case arises out of allegations of cartelisation in the tyre industry. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč All India Tyre Dealers‚Äô Federation (AITDF) was the complainant. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Allegation: 
97 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Despite a sharp fall in raw material prices (natural rubber, crude oil derivatives), 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Tyre manufacturers simultaneously increased tyre prices. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Entities involved included major tyre manufacturers such as: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CEAT, MRF, Apollo, JK Tyre, Birla Tyres, etc. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Two trade associations were involved: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
ATMA (Automotive Tyre Manufacturers‚Äô Association) ‚Äď representing domestic manufacturers. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
AITMF (All India Tyre Manufacturers‚Äô Federation) ‚Äď perceived to be aligned more with importers and 
MNCs. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Importantly, these associations were not acting in concert and were often adversarial. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Evidence relied upon by the CCI: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč One email trail allegedly showing coordination. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Statistical analysis by the DG: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Degree of price increase, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Correlation coefficient to establish parallel pricing behaviour. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Procedural background: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč AITDF had earlier filed a complaint before the MRTP Commission (RTPE No. 20 of 2008). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The CCI dismissed similar allegations in 2012 (Final Order dated 30.10.2012). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč COMPAT dismissed the appeal in 2013. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In the present case: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The CCI did not initiate suo motu action. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Investigation was initiated pursuant to a reference under Section 19(1)(b) from the Ministry of Corporate Affairs 
(MCA), which forwarded AITDF‚Äôs representation in 2013. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI Order (31.08.2018): 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Found violation of Section 3(3)(a) and Section 3(3)(b) read with Section 3(1). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Imposed penalties on tyre companies and certain individuals. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 14 appeals were filed before the NCLAT against this order, heard together. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether conscious price parallelism in the tyre industry amounted to cartelisation under Section 3(3) of the Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the DG‚Äôs statistical findings were reliable, or vitiated by arithmetical and methodological errors. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the CCI established ‚Äúplus factors‚ÄĚ beyond price parallelism to infer an agreement. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether mere existence of industry platforms / associations and email communication establishes concerted action. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether penalties under Section 48 could be imposed on individuals, particularly persons not employed during the period 
of contravention. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Statutory Provisions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(1) ‚ÄĒ Prohibits agreements causing AAEC. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(3)(a) ‚ÄĒ Price fixing agreements (presumed AAEC). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(3)(b) ‚ÄĒ Limiting or controlling production or supply. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 19(1)(b) ‚ÄĒ Reference by Central Government. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 27 ‚ÄĒ Penalties on enterprises. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 48 ‚ÄĒ Liability of persons in charge of conduct of company. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 15(a) & (b) ‚ÄĒ Vacancy or defect in CCI constitution does not vitiate proceedings. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Jurisprudential Principles 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conscious parallelism ‚Č† cartel (LPG Cylinder / Rajasthan Cylinders line of cases). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Plus factors required to infer agreement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Cartelisation must be proven by meeting of minds, not mere market outcomes. 
 
98 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Price Parallelism & Industry Behaviour 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conscious price parallelism is common in oligopolistic markets, especially where: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Demand elasticity is low; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Firms face similar cost structures and external constraints. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In such markets: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Firms may mirror competitors‚Äô prices to survive. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Price parallelism may be a rational business response, not collusion. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč This furthers the LPG Cylinders jurisprudence, recognising that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Identical or similar pricing outcomes alone do not establish cartelisation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Plus Factors ‚ÄĒ Critical Contribution of the Case 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The NCLAT explicitly articulated the need for ‚Äúplus factors‚ÄĚ: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Price parallelism plus additional incriminating circumstances is required to attract Section 3. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Examples of plus factors (implicitly recognised): 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Explicit communication evidencing agreement; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Control over output or supply; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Market allocation; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Evidence of coercion or enforcement mechanisms. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs failure: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Could not extract a clear plus factor from the email relied upon. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The email was not explicit enough to demonstrate agreement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Additional contextual indicators negating collusion: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Firms lost market share despite alleged cartel conduct. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Domestic manufacturers faced competitive pressure from imports. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Manufacturers lacked effective control over production, given dependence on imports and global supply. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Errors in DG‚Äôs Statistical Analysis 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The DG committed serious arithmetical and methodological errors, including: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Incorrect calculation of price increase: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
DG stated increase was ~11%; 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Correct figures showed a range of 11.6%‚Äď16.5%, which is too wide to indicate coordinated 
conduct. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Incorrect financial period used for correlation coefficient: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Used FY 2009‚Äď13 instead of FY 2011‚Äď12. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Corrected data revealed: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Non-existence of close price parallelism. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Low correlation coefficient, undermining inference of coordination. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The NCLAT rejected the CCI‚Äôs characterisation of these errors as merely ‚Äútypographical‚ÄĚ. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3(3)(b): Limiting Production or Supply 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The AITDF representation and MCA reference did not allege output restriction. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The CCI‚Äôs order was silent on evidence of limiting supply. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, finding under Section 3(3)(b) was unsustainable ‚Üí set aside. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Role of Trade Associations & Communication Platforms 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Mere existence of functional platforms or associations does not imply concerted action. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Industry meetings may legitimately discuss: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Cost reduction; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Efficiency; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Policy concerns. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Remand to CCI 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since cartel finding for FY 2011‚Äď12 was: 
99 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Heavily reliant on flawed calculations, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Surrounded by evidentiary and analytical errors, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT remitted the matter to the CCI to: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Re-examine statistical analysis, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Reconsider penalties, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Keep in mind pressure on domestic industry from global manufacturers. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Individual Liability under Section 48 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Nitish Bajaj (Vice President, Marketing ‚Äď CEAT) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CCI imposed a penalty under Section 27 read with Section 48. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Defence: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
He was not employed with CEAT in 2011, the year of alleged contravention. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Though raised for the first time at appellate stage, NCLAT accepted the defence. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Held: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 48 applies only to persons in charge at the time of contravention. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Penalty on a non-employee is impermissible. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Penalty set aside. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Other Individuals (e.g., Alok Somani, Bhupesh Bafna) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Multiple emails and WhatsApp communications showed: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Active involvement in cartel arrangements; 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Coordination regarding tenders and pricing. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Held liable under: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 48(1) ‚ÄĒ in charge of conduct of business; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 48(2) ‚ÄĒ consent, connivance, or neglect. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Penalties upheld. 
 
Rajasthan Cylinders v. CCI [2018 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Originated from Pankaj Gas Cylinders v. Indian Oil Corporation Ltd. (IOCL). While examining this complaint, CCI, in 
exercise of its statutory obligation under Section 18, expanded the scope of inquiry beyond the individual complainant ‚Üí 
felt that investigation was necessary in the case of all bidders who were the suppliers of 14.2 kg LPG cylinders. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Concerned a tender floated by IOCL in the year 2010‚Äď11 for the supply of 105 lakh LPG cylinders of 14.2 kg capacity with 
SC valves ‚Üí manufactured by a set of suppliers who catered almost exclusively to three public sector oil marketing 
companies, namely IOCL, Bharat Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (BPCL), and Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. 
(HPCL) + no alternative buyers for these cylinders in the market. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DG observed that 50 empanelled LPG cylinder manufacturers participated in the tender process and that their bids 
displayed a striking pattern of similarity, notwithstanding differences in their locations, scale of production, and cost 
structures. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Relied on a combination of market characteristics and conduct-based indicators: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Presence of market conditions conducive to collusion 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A small number of suppliers 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Limited new entry,  
‚Ė†‚Äč
The existence of an active trade association,  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Repetitive tendering,  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Identical products with no substitutes,  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Absence of technological change, and  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Indian LPG Cylinders Manufacturers Association, of which many of the bidders were members ‚Üí found 
to be active, and nineteen of its members attended a meeting in Mumbai on 3 March 2010, merely two 
days prior to the submission of tenders ‚Üí enabled bidders to coordinate their prices and ensured that 
100 
 
each member received a portion of the order, thereby preventing new entrants from independently 
quoting competitive prices. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Several suppliers had appointed common agents to submit bids on their behalf and that bids remained 
identical despite varying costs. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Findings of CCI and COMPAT 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The suppliers had engaged in bid-rigging, a finding later upheld by the COMPAT, though COMPAT reduced the 
penalties imposed.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Both authorities placed strong reliance on the identical or near-identical bids submitted by all 50 suppliers, the 
existence of an active trade association, the Mumbai meeting shortly before bid submission, and the appointment 
of common agents.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The presumption under Section 3(3) was invoked, and it was held that the conduct had resulted in AAEC. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the conduct of the LPG cylinder manufacturers amounted to bid-rigging or cartelisation under Section 3(3)(d) of 
the Competition Act? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the evidence on record was sufficient to establish the existence of an agreement or concerted practice? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether such conduct resulted in an AAEC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the statutory presumption under Section 3(3) stood was rebutted in light of the peculiar market structure and the 
role of IOCL in price determination? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3(3) of the Competition Act, 2002 ‚Äď  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Dyestuffs / Woodpulp (Ahlstr√∂m) test ‚Üí agreement may be inferred only where concertation is the only plausible 
explanation for the impugned conduct. Where the conduct is equally consistent with lawful competitive behaviour, no 
inference of collusion can be drawn.  
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Arguments Before the Supreme Court 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Competition Act presupposes a competitive market, whereas the LPG cylinder market was heavily regulated 
and characterised by oligopsony.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč With only three buyers, and IOCL being the largest with a market share of approximately 48%, the buyers 
exercised decisive control over prices.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Bid prices had little sanctity, as contracts were awarded not only to the L1 bidder but also to L2 and L3 bidders at 
the same negotiated rate.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
The final prices were based not on quoted bids but on IOCL‚Äôs internal cost estimates, followed by 
negotiations. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Supreme Court undertook a detailed examination of market structure and concluded that the market exhibited features 
of oligopsony. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč If IOCL, BPCL, and HPCL did not purchase from a supplier, that supplier would have no alternative outlet, 
effectively forcing exit from the market.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč IOCL exercised substantial influence over price determination, as it prepared an internal benchmark price 
(‚āĻ1106.61 per cylinder), adjusted for freight and other components, and awarded contracts at prices lower than 
this benchmark after negotiations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč IOCL ensured that all technically viable bidders were awarded some quantity, across all States, in order to maintain 
supply continuity of an essential public good.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Had IOCL excluded some manufacturers entirely, they might have shut down operations, which would 
be against IOCL‚Äôs long-term procurement interests. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Rebuttal of Collusion Inference 
101 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč While acknowledging that certain factors pointed towards collusive bidding (such as identical prices), these were 
insufficient because an equally, if not more, plausible explanation existed, namely IOCL‚Äôs pervasive control over 
price formation.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč In markets with a small number of buyers, parallel pricing does not automatically imply concerted practice, 
especially where suppliers lack pricing autonomy. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Court also rejected the reliance on the Mumbai meeting as decisive evidence.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Only 19 bidders attended the meeting, yet even non-attendees and non-members of the trade association quoted 
nearly identical prices ‚Üí the reason for uniform pricing was structural and not the result of cartel coordination. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The CCI‚Äôs treatment of repetitive bidding and identical products as ‚Äúplus factors‚ÄĚ was similarly rejected.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Repetitive bidding was inevitable given the nature of annual tenders, and identical products naturally limited price 
variation 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Such factors could not be treated as incriminating in isolation from the economic context. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A major flaw identified by the Court was that the CCI never summoned IOCL, despite IOCL‚Äôs central role in determining 
prices.  
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The suppliers had successfully discharged the burden of rebutting the presumption under Section 3(3).  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Court found no sufficient evidence of an agreement or concerted practice, holding that concertation was not the only 
plausible explanation for the identical bids. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Consequently, the orders of the CCI and COMPAT were set aside, and since no penalty survived, the appeals filed 
by the CCI on penalty were rendered infructuous. 
 
Re Delhi Jal Board and Grasim Industries Limited [2017 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The case arises from allegations of collusive tendering and bid-rigging in the procurement of chemicals used for water 
purification by the DJB.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč For the production of potable water, three chemicals are widely used: Poly Aluminium Chloride (PAC), Alum, and 
Liquid Chlorine (LC).  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Among these, PAC is predominantly used due to its ease of handling. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DJB regularly procured PAC (a coagulant) and LC (a disinfectant) through a competitive tendering process from various 
chemical manufacturers.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Opposite Parties (OPs) included: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Grasim Industries Limited (GIL) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Aditya Birla Chemicals (India) Limited (ABCIL) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Gujarat Alkalies and Chemicals Limited (GACL) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Kanoria Chemicals and Industries Limited (KCIL) (PAC tenders only) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Punjab Alkalis & Chemicals Limited (PACL) (LC tenders only) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DJB alleged that from 2006‚Äď07 to 2012, the OPs: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Quoted similar bid prices, differing marginally by only ‚āĻ200‚Äď400 for specific quantities. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč During negotiations, reduced bid prices by exactly the same amount, suggesting prior coordination. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Decided collectively how much the bid price should be reduced during negotiations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Effectively vitiated the tendering process, leaving DJB with no option but to accept cartel-determined prices. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Based on these allegations, DJB made two statutory references under Section 19(1)(b) of the Competition Act, 2002: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reference Case No. 03/2013: Alleging collusive tendering in PAC procurement by GIL, ABCIL, GACL, and 
KCIL. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reference Case No. 04/2013: Alleging collusive tendering in LC procurement by GIL, ABCIL, GACL (replaced by 
PACL), and others. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DG investigated both references and concluded that the OPs had acted collusively in PAC tenders, contravening Section 3(1) 
read with Section 3(3)(d) of the Act, though no contravention was established against KCIL. 
102 
 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the conduct of the OPs in bidding for DJB‚Äôs PAC tenders amounted to collusive tendering/bid-rigging under 
Section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act, 2002. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether similar conduct could be established in respect of LC tenders. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether coordination between subsidiaries of the same parent company (ABCIL and GIL) could fall within the ambit of 
Section 3. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether sufficient ‚Äúplus factors‚ÄĚ existed to establish a meeting of minds and concerted action. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč What should be the quantum of penalty, if any, and whether public interest considerations are relevant. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether GACL‚Äôs conduct met the standard of proof required to establish cartelisation. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3(1): Prohibits agreements that cause or are likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect on competition (AAEC) in 
India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3(3)(d): Deems bid-rigging or collusive bidding as per se anti-competitive. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 19(1)(b): Empowers statutory authorities to make references to the CCI. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Cartel jurisprudence: Requires proof of concerted action, often inferred through circumstantial evidence and ‚Äúplus factors‚ÄĚ 
such as price parallelism combined with information exchange, lack of economic rationale, bid rotation, or 
supra-competitive pricing. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Group concept under the Act: Relevant only for combinations (Sections 5 and 6), not for agreements under Section 3. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PAC Tenders 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Commission undertook a detailed economic and behavioural analysis and made several crucial observations: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Price Parallelism and Convergence ‚Äď examination of PAC rates from 2009‚Äď10 to 2014‚Äď15 showed that 
prices: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Increased simultaneously every year, and 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Converged into a narrow pricing band, despite varying cost structures. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Geographical and Freight Cost Anomalies 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Although the plants of the OPs were located in different geographical regions, their quoted 
prices remained substantially similar. Contrary to market logic: 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Freight rates did not decrease with distance. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
GACL, despite being farthest from DJB, quoted the highest freight rate. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Cost of Production Analysis 
‚óŹ‚Äč
GACL‚Äôs cost of production remained nearly constant but costs for GIL and ABCIL increased ‚Üí 
contradicted the OPs‚Äô defence that narrow pricing was due to PAC being a homogeneous 
product. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Discriminatory Pricing ‚Üí the OPs charged higher prices to DJB than to other customers ‚Üí could not be 
adequately justified by ABCIL, GIL, or GACL. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Exchange of Sensitive Information ‚Üí particularly between GIL and ABCIL, there was evidence of: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Sharing bid documents, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Discussing prices to be quoted, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Coordinating negotiated prices. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Behaviour Across Other Municipal Tenders ‚Äď similar pricing patterns were observed in tenders floated by other 
municipal corporations, strengthening the inference of cartel behaviour. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Plus Factors and Meeting of Minds ‚Üí the Commission held that: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Close price margins despite cost variations, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Prolonged supra-competitive pricing, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Bid rotation, 
103 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Exchange of vital information, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Lack of economic justification 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Taken cumulatively, constituted sufficient plus factors to establish concerted action. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč LC Tenders ‚Äď the Commission took a different view for LC tenders: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč LC is a by-product, hazardous and toxic, with different market conditions from PAC. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Apart from similar bid submission timing, no strong indicators of coordination were found. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The DG failed to analyse basic price, transportation costs, taxes, and profit margins. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč In the absence of such analysis, no contravention could be established. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Group Defence 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ABCIL and GIL argued that as subsidiaries of the same parent company, they formed a ‚Äúgroup‚ÄĚ and could not be 
competitors. The Commission rejected this defence, holding that: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
In tendering, they were treated as independent bidders. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The group concept is confined to combination regulation, not Section 3 agreements. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The CCI held that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ABCIL, GIL, and GACL had acted in a concerted manner in DJB‚Äôs PAC tenders from 2009‚Äď10 to 2014‚Äď15, 
violating Section 3(1) read with Section 3(3)(d). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No contravention was established against KCIL. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No violation was found in respect of LC tenders, and Reference Case No. 04/2013 was closed. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Penalty ‚Üí Considering that PAC is essential for water purification and directly impacts public health, the Commission 
treated this as an aggravating factor and imposed penalties based on average relevant turnover: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ABCIL: 8% ‚Äď ‚āĻ2.09 crore 
‚ěĘ‚Äč GIL: 8% ‚Äď ‚āĻ2.30 crore 
‚ěĘ‚Äč GACL: 6% ‚Äď ‚āĻ1.88 crore 
 
Dissenting Opinion ‚Üí Mr. Sudhir Mittal, Member, CCI, dissented insofar as GACL was concerned. He held that: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The evidence against GACL did not sufficiently meet the standard of proof required in cartel cases. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The alleged conduct of GACL did not adequately fit within the established ‚Äúplus factors‚ÄĚ framework. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Accordingly, he did not find GACL liable for contravention of Section 3(3)(d)
key_principles: ‚Äč Samir Agrawal, an independent legal practitioner, filed information before the CCI alleging cartelisation by drivers of Ola
‚Äč The allegation was premised on algorithmic pricing used by Ola and Uber, which:
‚Äč Prevented riders from negotiating fares with drivers;
‚Äč Prevented drivers from offering discounts or competing on price;
‚Äč Imposed a uniform price determined by the platform‚Äôs algorithm.
‚Äč According to the informant:
‚Äč The algorithm artificially manipulated supply and demand;
‚Äč It guaranteed higher fares to drivers who would otherwise compete with one another;
‚Äč This resulted in price fixation and restriction of competition.
‚Äč Alleged violation: Section 3(1) read with Section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act, 2002.
‚Äč The informant argued that:
‚Äč Ola and Uber acted as the ‚Äúhub‚ÄĚ, and
‚Äč The drivers constituted the ‚Äúspokes‚ÄĚ, collectively forming a hub-and-spoke cartel.
‚Äč The argument relied heavily on foreign antitrust jurisprudence, particularly a US class action (Spencer Meyer v.
‚Äč Procedural History
‚Äč CCI‚Äôs Findings ‚Üí no agreement or cartelisation was established.
‚Äč Hub-and-spoke theory rejected:
‚Äč Section 4 (abuse of dominance) ‚Üí no dominant position established ‚áí hence, no inquiry under Section 4 was
‚Äč Whether the alleged conduct amounted to a ‚Äúhub and spoke cartel‚ÄĚ under the Competition Act, 2002.
‚Äč Whether there was an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ or meeting of minds between drivers to fix prices.
